AlgoSec Diagnostic Playbooks

Target: algosec-lab | Host: algosec | Generated: 2026-03-30T19:51:13Z

Use this page to get to the right playbook quickly, run the next command, validate the output, and isolate the likely failure point.

System baseline

OS: Rocky Linux 8.10 (Green Obsidian)

Kernel: 4.18.0-553.89.1.el8_10.x86_64

Runtime footprint

Services observed: 261

Product services: 29

Support focus

Critical services: 27

Listening endpoints: 52

Jump To The Right Playbook

Scan the symptom, read the start clue, and open the playbook that best matches what the customer is reporting.

ASMS UI is downOpen: ASMS UI is downStart here: Check whether the host is under storage, inode, memory, or CPU pressure before blaming the ASMS UI path.UI downloginhttpd80/443keycloakmetroASMS login page not loadingOpen: ASMS UI is downStart here: Check whether the host is under storage, inode, memory, or CPU pressure before blaming the ASMS UI path.UI downloginhttpd80/443keycloakmetroFireFlow action failingOpen: FireFlow BackendStart here: Confirm aff-boot.service is active and /FireFlow/api or /aff/api still proxy to localhost:1989.FireFlow/FireFlow/api1989aff-bootpostgresFireFlow API errorOpen: FireFlow BackendStart here: Confirm aff-boot.service is active and /FireFlow/api or /aff/api still proxy to localhost:1989.FireFlow/FireFlow/api1989aff-bootpostgresFeature failing behind UIOpen: Microservice PlatformStart here: Confirm algosec-ms.service completed successfully and the top ms-* services are active.feature failsms-*routesms-configurationSpecific microservice path failingOpen: Microservice PlatformStart here: Confirm algosec-ms.service completed successfully and the top ms-* services are active.feature failsms-*routesms-configurationJob stuck or not progressingOpen: Messaging and DataStart here: Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present.job stuckqueueactivemq61616postgresData-backed action failingOpen: Messaging and DataStart here: Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present.job stuckqueueactivemq61616postgres

ASMS UI is down

Use this when the ASMS UI is down and the engineer is checking the appliance from SSH.

Back to top
Dependency path
Step 1Host sanity gate for ASMS runtimeStart here with a short sanity gate. Check storage, inode, memory, OOM, load, and CPU pressure to decide whether the Rocky host can still support the ASMS runtime and its supporting services before you deepen the ASMS path itself.Step 2Apache/HTTPD serving the UIIf the host looks healthy, confirm Apache/HTTPD can answer local UI traffic and still route requests into the auth and app branches.Step 3Core ASMS services and safe restart boundaryIf the host and Apache edge look healthy, stay shallow first. Confirm the core ASMS services that support the UI are up, listening, and restartable: `httpd.service`, `keycloak.service`, and `ms-metro.service`. Use restart steps only for the service whose shallow check actually failed or hung.Step 4First usable shell and case classificationUse the customer-visible shell boundary to decide whether the case is still `GUI down`. If the login page loads or the user reaches a usable home shell, stop treating it as a top-level UI outage and branch into the narrower failing workflow.Step 5Escalation-only deeper auth and app correlationOnly if the shallow host, Apache, service, and first-shell checks still do not explain the case should you replay the login bootstrap, auth chain, or deeper module seams. Those are escalation paths, not the frontline default.
Step 1
Step focus

Check whether the host can support useful work for the ASMS UI path. Confirm storage, inode, memory, load, OOM, and CPU pressure are not already blocking Apache, Keycloak, Metro, log writing, or temp-file work.

Run
Check storage pressure on runtime filesystems
df -h
Healthy means: Runtime filesystems still have enough free space for logs, temp files, and service work.
Why this check matters
  • This checks human-readable disk usage for the main filesystems.
  • Focus on `Use%` and `Avail`, especially for `/` and `/data`.
  • If disk is full, services can fail to write logs, temp files, or runtime data.
Known-good example
Filesystem           Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/mapper/rl-root   60G   16G   45G  27% /
/dev/mapper/rl-data  238G   21G  218G   9% /data
Check inode pressure on runtime filesystems
df -ih
Healthy means: Runtime filesystems still have free inodes for logs, temp files, sockets, and service output.
Why this check matters
  • This checks inode usage, which is different from normal disk space.
  • A filesystem can still have free space but fail because it has no free inodes left.
  • Focus on `IUse%` and whether `IFree` is close to zero.
Known-good example
Filesystem          Inodes IUsed IFree IUse% Mounted on
/dev/mapper/rl-root    30M  343K   30M    2% /
/dev/mapper/rl-data   119M   21K  119M    1% /data
Check memory pressure on JVM-backed services
free -h
Healthy means: Available memory is still present and swap is not carrying active pressure for the host.
Why this check matters
  • This is the quick memory check for the host.
  • Focus on `available` memory and whether swap is starting to grow heavily.
  • Low available memory can slow down or crash Java services before the whole server looks down.
Known-good example
              total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           32Gi        13Gi       8.6Gi       2.4Gi         9Gi        15Gi
Swap:          24Gi          0B        24Gi
Check current host load pressure
uptime
Healthy means: Load is not unexpectedly high for the host and does not already explain the UI symptom.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
14:42:03 up 17 days,  1:52,  1 user,  load average: 0.25, 0.18, 0.11
Check recent memory-kill pressure
journalctl -k --since "24 hours ago" --no-pager | grep -i -E "out of memory|oom|killed process"
Healthy means: No recent Out Of Memory lines are returned.
Why this check matters
  • This checks the Linux kernel log for memory-pressure kills.
  • OOM means Out Of Memory. Linux may kill a process to protect the server.
  • If you see OOM or `Killed process` lines, memory pressure is likely part of the failure.
Known-good example
No output
Check which process owns CPU pressure right now
ps -eo pid,comm,%cpu,%mem --sort=-%cpu | head -n 10
Healthy means: No unexpected process is consuming enough CPU to starve the rest of the ASMS path.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
  PID COMMAND         %CPU %MEM
 6012 java             8.1  7.4
 1018 httpd            1.2  0.4
 3758 algosec_keycloa  0.8  1.6
Step 2
Step focus

Check whether Apache/HTTPD can do useful edge work for the ASMS UI path. Confirm httpd.service is running, 80 and 443 are listening, the suite login route still lands on the Apache-served UI page, representative /algosec-ui/ assets still load through Apache, and the proxy mapping still points toward the auth and app branches.

Run
Check httpd.service status
systemctl status httpd.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Known-good example
● httpd.service - The Apache HTTP Server
   Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/httpd.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:37:12 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 1018 (/usr/sbin/httpd)
Check edge listeners
ss -lntp | grep -E ':(80|443)\b'
Healthy means: A listening socket is present for 80, 443.
Why this check matters
  • This command shows whether Linux is listening on the expected port.
  • `LISTEN` means a process has opened the port and is waiting for connections.
  • If the port is missing, the service may be down, starting slowly, or bound to the wrong place.
Known-good example
LISTEN 0 511 0.0.0.0:443 0.0.0.0:* users:(("/usr/sbin/httpd",pid=1018,fd=4),...)
LISTEN 0 511 0.0.0.0:80  0.0.0.0:* users:(("/usr/sbin/httpd",pid=1018,fd=3),...)
Check the legacy suite login route
curl -k -I --max-time 5 https://127.0.0.1/algosec/suite/login
Healthy means: Apache returns a redirect from the legacy suite login path into the current UI login page.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
HTTP/1.1 302 Found
Server: Apache
Location: https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login
Check the Apache-served UI login page
curl -k -I --max-time 5 https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login
Healthy means: Apache returns the current UI login page with HTTP 200.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: Apache
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Check representative /algosec-ui/ assets through Apache
for path in /algosec-ui/styles.css /algosec-ui/runtime.js /algosec-ui/main.js; do echo "=== $path ==="; curl -k -sS -D - -o /tmp/asms-ui-asset.out "https://127.0.0.1$path" | sed -n '1,8p'; printf 'BODY_BYTES '; wc -c < /tmp/asms-ui-asset.out; done
Healthy means: Apache returns HTTP 200 for representative CSS and JS assets and each body is non-empty.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
=== /algosec-ui/styles.css ===
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: Apache
Content-Type: text/css
BODY_BYTES 758180
=== /algosec-ui/runtime.js ===
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/javascript
BODY_BYTES 2737
Check Apache routes for login, auth, and app branches
grep -R -n -E 'algosec-ui|algosec/suite|ProxyPass|ProxyPassReverse|keycloak|8443|afa/api/v1|8080' /etc/httpd/conf.d 2>/dev/null
Healthy means: Apache config still shows the login redirect, the UI alias, and the auth and app route mapping needed by the ASMS UI path.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
78:RewriteRule "^/algosec/suite/login(.*)$" "/algosec-ui/login" [R]
299:AliasMatch (?i)^/algosec-ui/(.*)$ /usr/share/fa/suite/client/app/suite-new-ui/$1
1:<Location /keycloak/>
2:        ProxyPass https://localhost:8443/ timeout=300
139:<Location /afa/api/v1>
140:  ProxyPass http://localhost:8080/afa/api/v1 timeout=18000 retry=0
Step 3
Step focus

Check the core ASMS services that a frontline support engineer can actually verify and restart during a live customer session. Stay shallow here: `httpd.service`, `keycloak.service`, `ms-metro.service`, their expected listeners, and one service-specific restart only when the matching shallow check fails.

Run
Check httpd.service status
systemctl status httpd.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Known-good example
● httpd.service - The Apache HTTP Server
   Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/httpd.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Thu 2026-03-26 18:42:56 EDT; 17h ago
 Main PID: 1018 (/usr/sbin/httpd)
Check keycloak.service status
systemctl status keycloak.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Known-good example
● keycloak.service - Keycloak Service
   Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/keycloak.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Thu 2026-03-26 18:43:11 EDT; 17h ago
 Main PID: 2745 (java)
Check ms-metro.service status
systemctl status ms-metro.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Known-good example
● ms-metro.service - ms-metro Application Container
   Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/ms-metro.service; disabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:39:32 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 6012 (java)
Check the wrapper and listener surfaces once
systemctl status algosec-ms.service --no-pager; echo '--- listeners ---'; ss -lntp | grep -E ':(80|443|8080|8443)\b'
Healthy means: The wrapper unit is present, and the expected shallow listeners remain present on `80`, `443`, `8080`, and `8443`.
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Known-good example
● algosec-ms.service - AlgoSec Platform Wrapper
   Active: active (exited)
--- listeners ---
LISTEN 0 511 0.0.0.0:443 0.0.0.0:* users:(("/usr/sbin/httpd",pid=1018,fd=4),...)
LISTEN 0 100 0.0.0.0:8080 0.0.0.0:* users:(("java",pid=6012,fd=44))
LISTEN 0 100 0.0.0.0:8443 0.0.0.0:* users:(("java",pid=2745,fd=91))
Restart Apache only if the UI edge check failed
sudo systemctl restart httpd.service && sleep 5 && systemctl is-active httpd.service && curl -k -sS -I https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login | sed -n '1,8p'
Healthy means: Apache returns to `active`, and the login page immediately answers again through localhost HTTPS.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
active
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: Apache
Restart Keycloak only if the login page loads but auth service looks down
sudo systemctl restart keycloak.service && sleep 10 && systemctl is-active keycloak.service && ss -lntp | grep -E ':8443\b'
Healthy means: Keycloak returns to `active`, and the auth listener on `8443` is present again.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
active
LISTEN 0 100 0.0.0.0:8443 0.0.0.0:* users:(("java",pid=2745,fd=91))
Restart Metro only if the UI reaches the app shell but it is blank or partly loaded
sudo systemctl restart ms-metro.service && sleep 10 && systemctl is-active ms-metro.service && curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:8080/afa/getStatus --max-time 10
Healthy means: Metro returns to `active`, and the heartbeat shows `"isAlive" : true`.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
active
{
  "isAlive" : true
}
Step 4
Step focus

Check the first usable ASMS shell only after the edge and core service checks are already healthy. Use the rendered login or home shell to decide whether this is still `GUI down` or whether the user has already crossed into a narrower app workflow problem.

Run
Check ms-metro.service status
systemctl status ms-metro.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Known-good example
● ms-metro.service - ms-metro Application Container
   Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/ms-metro.service; disabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:39:32 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 6012 (java)
Check Metro listener
ss -lntp | grep -E ':8080\b'
Healthy means: Port 8080 is listening for ms-metro.
Why this check matters
  • This command shows whether Linux is listening on the expected port.
  • `LISTEN` means a process has opened the port and is waiting for connections.
  • If the port is missing, the service may be down, starting slowly, or bound to the wrong place.
Known-good example
LISTEN 0 100 0.0.0.0:8080 0.0.0.0:* users:(("java",pid=6012,fd=44))
Compare the first usable shell gate with Metro bootstrap clues
minute=$(grep -E '/afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php|/afa/php/home.php|dynamic\.js\.php|/afa/php/home.js' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 1 | sed -E 's/.*\[([^]]{17})[^]]*\].*/\1/'); printf 'WINDOW=%s\n' "$minute"; printf '=== apache shell minute ===\n'; grep "$minute" /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | grep -E '/afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php|/afa/php/home.php|dynamic\.js\.php|/afa/php/home.js' | tail -n 20; printf '=== metro same minute ===\n'; grep "$minute" /data/ms-metro/logs/localhost_access_log.txt | grep -E '/afa/getStatus|/afa/api/v1/config\?|/afa/api/v1/session/extend|/afa/api/v1/config/all/noauth' | tail -n 20
Healthy means: The same shell-transition minute shows `SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php -> /afa/php/home.php -> dynamic.js.php/home.js` on the Apache side and the nearest Metro bootstrap clues such as `GET /afa/getStatus`, `GET /afa/api/v1/config?...`, `POST /afa/api/v1/session/extend?...`, or `GET /afa/api/v1/config/all/noauth?...` on the Metro side.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
WINDOW=25/Mar/2026:17:29
=== apache shell minute ===
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false HTTP/1.1" 200 65
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:09 -0400] "GET /afa/php/home.php HTTP/1.1" 200 35713
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:10 -0400] "GET /afa/php/JSlib1768164240/dynamic.js.php?sid=abc123fresh HTTP/1.1" 200 23846
=== metro same minute ===
127.0.0.1 [25/Mar/2026:17:29:35 -0400] [http-nio-0.0.0.0-8080-exec-7] "POST /afa/api/v1/session/extend?session=abc123fresh&domain=0 HTTP/1.1" 200 - 246 33410 -
127.0.0.1 [25/Mar/2026:17:29:35 -0400] [http-nio-0.0.0.0-8080-exec-8] "GET /afa/api/v1/config/all/noauth?domain=0 HTTP/1.1" 200 812 1543 33411 -
127.0.0.1 [25/Mar/2026:17:29:43 -0400] [http-nio-0.0.0.0-8080-exec-9] "GET /afa/getStatus HTTP/1.1" 200 33 506 33738 -
Check post-home shell and dashboard hydration traffic
printf '=== apache ===\n'; grep -E '/afa/php/home.php|dynamic\.js\.php|/afa/php/commands.php\?cmd=DISPLAY_ISSUES_CENTER|/fa/tree/create|/afa/php/prod_stat.php|/fa/tree/get_update|FireFlowBridge.js|/afa/php/logo.php|/afa/api/v1/license|/ms-watchdog/v1/api/issues-center/issues/countAll' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 60; printf '=== metro ===\n'; grep -E '/afa/getStatus|/afa/api/v1/bridge/refresh|/afa/api/v1/license|/afa/api/v1/config\?|/afa/api/v1/session/extend|/afa/api/v1/config/all/noauth' /data/ms-metro/logs/localhost_access_log.txt | tail -n 30
Healthy means: Recent lines show `/afa/php/home.php`, the first dashboard-hydration routes such as `dynamic.js.php`, `DISPLAY_ISSUES_CENTER`, `/fa/tree/create`, `/afa/php/prod_stat.php`, or `/fa/tree/get_update`, and the nearby Metro clues such as `GET /afa/getStatus`, `GET /afa/api/v1/license`, or the same-minute `config` and `session/extend` requests that surround the first usable shell.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
=== apache ===
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:17:29:38 -0400] "GET /afa/php/home.php HTTP/1.1" 200 35714
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:13:14 -0400] "GET /afa/php/JSlib1768164240/dynamic.js.php?sid=nqo0c2b0dda9d9a4gas11vc6h6 HTTP/1.1" 200 23846
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:13:15 -0400] "GET /afa/php/commands.php?cmd=DISPLAY_ISSUES_CENTER HTTP/1.1" 200 741
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:13:15 -0400] "POST /fa/tree/create HTTP/1.1" 200 4211
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:13:19 -0400] "GET /afa/php/prod_stat.php HTTP/1.1" 200 169
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:13:19 -0400] "POST /fa/tree/get_update HTTP/1.1" 200 1642
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:17:29:39 -0400] "GET /afa/php/JSlib1768164240/FireFlowBridge.js HTTP/1.1" 200 484
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:17:29:39 -0400] "GET /afa/php/logo.php HTTP/1.1" 200 -
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:17:29:36 -0400] "GET /afa/api/v1/license HTTP/1.1" 200 311
=== metro ===
127.0.0.1 [25/Mar/2026:17:29:35 -0400] [http-nio-0.0.0.0-8080-exec-7] "POST /afa/api/v1/session/extend?session=abc123fresh&domain=0 HTTP/1.1" 200 - 246 33410 -
127.0.0.1 [25/Mar/2026:17:29:35 -0400] [http-nio-0.0.0.0-8080-exec-8] "GET /afa/api/v1/config/all/noauth?domain=0 HTTP/1.1" 200 812 1543 33411 -
127.0.0.1 [25/Mar/2026:17:29:43 -0400] [http-nio-0.0.0.0-8080-exec-9] "GET /afa/getStatus HTTP/1.1" 200 33 506 33738 -
127.0.0.1 [25/Mar/2026:17:29:44 -0400] [http-nio-0.0.0.0-8080-exec-8] "POST /afa/api/v1/bridge/refresh?session=kk4msqvndoc0c8lmjka8i93vj4&domain=0 HTTP/1.1" 200 - 1739954 33412 -
Check whether the case already crossed into a later content branch
grep -E '/fa/tree/create|/afa/php/commands.php\?cmd=(GET_REPORTS|GET_POLICY_TAB|GET_DEVICE_POLICY|GET_MONITORING_CHANGES|GET_ANALYSIS_OPTIONS|GET_ANALYZE_DATA|ANALYZE)' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 40
Healthy means: Recent Apache lines show the device tree refresh plus any later content-branch markers such as `GET_REPORTS`, `GET_POLICY_TAB`, `GET_DEVICE_POLICY`, `GET_MONITORING_CHANGES`, or `GET_ANALYSIS_OPTIONS`.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:13:15 -0400] "POST /fa/tree/create HTTP/1.1" 200 4211
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:16:42 -0400] "GET /afa/php/commands.php?cmd=GET_REPORTS HTTP/1.1" 200 812
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:18:09 -0400] "GET /afa/php/commands.php?cmd=GET_POLICY_TAB HTTP/1.1" 200 2213
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:18:09 -0400] "GET /afa/php/commands.php?cmd=GET_DEVICE_POLICY HTTP/1.1" 200 744
127.0.0.1 - - [26/Mar/2026:05:20:41 -0400] "GET /afa/php/commands.php?cmd=GET_ANALYSIS_OPTIONS HTTP/1.1" 200 1304
Check Metro heartbeat
curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:8080/afa/getStatus --max-time 10
Healthy means: The JSON response shows `"isAlive" : true`.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
{
  "isAlive" : true
}
Check Metro app traffic
grep -E '/afa/getStatus|/afa/api/v1/config\?|/afa/api/v1/license|/afa/api/v1/session/extend|/afa/api/v1/config/all/noauth' /data/ms-metro/logs/localhost_access_log.txt | tail -n 40
Healthy means: Recent access lines show normal 200 responses for the Metro heartbeat and the light authenticated `/afa/api/v1/...` paths that appear immediately after the home page loads.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Check what Metro is busy doing
ps -p $(cat /var/run/ms-metro/ms-metro.pid) -o pid,etime,%cpu,%mem,nlwp,cmd --cols 160
Healthy means: The Metro JVM is present, has a stable elapsed runtime, and its CPU, memory, and thread count look reasonable for the current case.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Check Metro JVM error clues
grep -n -i -E 'error|exception|failed|caused by|outofmemory|unable|refused|timed out' /data/ms-metro/logs/catalina.out | tail -n 40
Healthy means: No fresh Metro error signatures appear, or the returned lines clearly point to the real Java, dependency, or application failure.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Step 5
Step focus

If the shallow host, Apache, service, and first-shell checks still leave the stop point unclear, use one bounded reproduced journey plus logs to decide whether the deeper issue sits in login bootstrap, auth, or a later app seam.

Run
Replay one browser-like login journey with cookies before chasing neighbors
rm -f /tmp/asms-journey.cookies /tmp/asms-journey.headers /tmp/asms-journey.body; curl -k -sS -L -c /tmp/asms-journey.cookies -b /tmp/asms-journey.cookies -A 'Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/123.0.0.0 Safari/537.36' -H 'Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8' -H 'Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9' -H 'Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1' -D /tmp/asms-journey.headers -o /tmp/asms-journey.body https://127.0.0.1/algosec/suite/login; printf '=== headers ===\n'; sed -n '1,30p' /tmp/asms-journey.headers; printf '=== cookies ===\n'; cat /tmp/asms-journey.cookies 2>/dev/null; printf '=== html markers ===\n'; grep -E 'base href=|runtime.js|main.js|styles.css' /tmp/asms-journey.body | head -n 10
Healthy means: The replay proves the legacy suite redirect, the static shell markers, and whether the browser-like startup already picked up cookies before any deeper auth path is tested.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
HTTP/1.1 302 Found
Location: https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login
<base href="/algosec-ui/">
<script src="runtime.js?v=1768164271" type="module"></script>
<script src="main.js?v=1768164271" type="module"></script>
Correlate the reproduced minute in Apache access logs
grep -E '/algosec/suite/login|/algosec-ui/login|/algosec-ui/(styles.css|runtime.js|main.js)|/afa/php/commands.php\?cmd=IS_SESSION_ACTIVE|/afa/api/v1/config/PRINT_TABLE_IN_NORMAL_VIEW|/seikan/login/setup|/aff/api/internal/noauth/getStatus|/BusinessFlow|/afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php|/keycloak/|/FireFlow/SelfService/CheckAuthentication/|/afa/php/home.php|/afa/api/v1' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 120
Healthy means: Recent Apache access lines show the same reproduced journey and make it clear whether the request stopped at the shell, moved through the legacy setup and session-validation paths, and only later reached downstream modules such as BusinessFlow, Keycloak, FireFlow, the PHP home page, and Metro-backed app paths.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "GET /algosec-ui/login HTTP/1.1" 200 13345
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:00 -0400] "GET /seikan/login/setup HTTP/1.1" 200 217
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "GET /BusinessFlow/login HTTP/1.1" 302 30
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false HTTP/1.1" 200 65
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:02 -0400] "POST //keycloak/realms/master/protocol/openid-connect/token? HTTP/1.1" 200 1562
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:06 -0400] "POST /BusinessFlow/rest/v1/login HTTP/1.1" 200 49
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:09 -0400] "GET /afa/php/home.php HTTP/1.1" 200 35713
Review recent Apache/HTTPD logs
journalctl -u httpd.service -n 50 --no-pager
Healthy means: No obvious disk, permission, startup, or crash errors appear in recent lines.
Why this check matters
  • Recent service logs are often the fastest way to find the real failure clue.
  • Focus on startup errors, permission errors, heap errors, dependency failures, and repeated retries.
  • Use this after the status check when the service looks up but still behaves badly.
Review recent Keycloak logs
tail -n 80 /var/log/keycloak/keycloak.log
Healthy means: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear auth, database, TLS, or startup clue.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Review Metro error clues
grep -n -i -E 'error|exception|failed|caused by|outofmemory|unable|refused|timed out' /data/ms-metro/logs/catalina.out | tail -n 40
Healthy means: No fresh Metro error signatures appear, or the returned lines clearly point to the real Java, dependency, or application failure.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.

FireFlow Backend

Use this when a FireFlow action fails or FireFlow returns an error.

Back to top
Step 1
Step focus

Identify the failing FireFlow action and confirm that the symptom is tied to /FireFlow/api or /aff/api.

Run
Check config mapping
grep -n 1989 /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf
Healthy means: The expected mapping token 1989 appears in /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf.
Why this check matters
  • This checks whether the expected mapping still exists in the service config.
  • Use it to confirm the route, port, or target value is still what the application expects.
Step 2
Step focus

Confirm aff-boot.service is active and that the failing FireFlow route still proxies to localhost:1989.

Run
Check aff-boot.service status
systemctl status aff-boot.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Check FireFlow listener
ss -lntp | grep -E ':1989\b'
Healthy means: A listening socket is present for 1989.
Why this check matters
  • This command shows whether Linux is listening on the expected port.
  • `LISTEN` means a process has opened the port and is waiting for connections.
  • If the port is missing, the service may be down, starting slowly, or bound to the wrong place.
Check config mapping
grep -n 1989 /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf
Healthy means: The expected mapping token 1989 appears in /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf.
Why this check matters
  • This checks whether the expected mapping still exists in the service config.
  • Use it to confirm the route, port, or target value is still what the application expects.
Check the FireFlow session path behind BusinessFlow AFF connection
curl -sk -D - https://localhost/FireFlow/api/session | sed -n '1,12p'; echo '--- direct aff-boot ---'; curl -sk -D - https://localhost:1989/aff/api/external/session | sed -n '1,12p'
Healthy means: The Apache-fronted FireFlow session route and the direct aff-boot session route should return the same invalid-session shape.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Check aff-boot database and broker sockets
pid=$(systemctl show -p MainPID --value aff-boot.service); echo PID=$pid; lsof -Pan -p "$pid" -i 2>/dev/null | egrep '(:1989|:5432|:61616)' || true
Healthy means: The aff-boot Java PID still owns the 1989 listener and usually shows local PostgreSQL and broker connections for its live runtime.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Known-good example
PID=1687
java 1687 root   24u  IPv6 ... TCP *:1989 (LISTEN)
java 1687 root  146u  IPv6 ... TCP 127.0.0.1:20770->127.0.0.1:5432 (ESTABLISHED)
java 1687 root  165u  IPv6 ... TCP 127.0.0.1:21910->127.0.0.1:61616 (ESTABLISHED)
Step 3
Step focus

Confirm postgresql.service is active and local database access is healthy before assuming a FireFlow-only fault.

Run
Check postgresql.service status
systemctl status postgresql.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Step 4
Step focus

Collect the latest FireFlow-related error lines or status output and decide whether the failure stays in FireFlow logic or must move deeper into dependencies.

Run
Review aff-boot.service logs
journalctl -u aff-boot.service -n 50 --no-pager
Healthy means: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.
Why this check matters
  • Recent service logs are often the fastest way to find the real failure clue.
  • Focus on startup errors, permission errors, heap errors, dependency failures, and repeated retries.
  • Use this after the status check when the service looks up but still behaves badly.
Check same-minute configuration refresh clues
grep -E 'AlgoSec_FireFlow|AlgoSec_ApplicationDiscovery|swagger|BAD_GATEWAY' /data/algosec-ms/logs/ms-configuration.log | tail -n 40; echo '--- apache ---'; grep -E 'AutoDiscovery|swagger/v2/api-docs' /var/log/httpd/ssl_error_log | tail -n 20
Healthy means: Recent lines either stay quiet or show whether the failing FireFlow minute rolled into unified swagger refresh, downstream service-definition fetches, or a concrete Apache-side 502.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Check whether CommandsDispatcher stayed on journal or session maintenance
grep -E 'CommandsDispatcher|/journal/getChangesInOrigRulesByDate|/FireFlow/api/session|/session/extend' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 60
Healthy means: The returned window shows whether the FireFlow branch stayed on journal refresh and session maintenance or moved into a different workflow shape.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Check whether the branch is config broadcast plus FireFlow auth bootstrap
grep -E 'application-afaConfig.properties|notify ActiveMq broadcast|MicroserviceConfigurationBroadcast|Refreshing application context' /data/algosec-ms/logs/ms-configuration.log /data/algosec-ms/logs/ms-initial-plan.log | tail -n 60; echo '--- fireflow ---'; grep -E 'authenticateUser|GetUserInfo' /usr/share/fireflow/var/log/fireflow.log /usr/share/fireflow/var/log/fireflow.log.1 /usr/share/fireflow/var/log/fireflow.log.2 2>/dev/null | tail -n 40
Healthy means: The returned window shows whether the same FireFlow minute is really a config-broadcast ripple with nearby authentication and user bootstrap rather than a ticket-progression branch.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.
Check whether FireFlow is active but the lab has zero tickets
grep -E 'Total tickets in DB|tickets updated in the last 10 minutes|setup/fireflow/is_enabled|allowedDevices|brandConfig' /usr/share/fireflow/var/log/fireflow.log /usr/share/fireflow/var/log/fireflow.log.1 /usr/share/fireflow/var/log/fireflow.log.2 2>/dev/null | tail -n 80; echo '--- apache ---'; grep -E '/setup/fireflow/is_enabled|/allowedDevices|/bridge/refresh|/config/all/noauth' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 40
Healthy means: The returned window shows whether FireFlow is enabled and polling neighboring dependencies successfully while still reporting zero tickets in DB.
Why this check matters
  • This command gives a focused check for the current step.
  • Use the healthy example below as the main output reference for what good looks like.

Microservice Platform

Use this when one product feature fails after login and the UI itself is still up.

Back to top
Step 1
Step focus

Identify the exact failing feature or ms-* path in the customer session so the diagnostic path stays anchored to one concrete product behavior.

Step 2
Step focus

Confirm algosec-ms.service completed successfully and that the likely target service set is healthy: ms-batch-application.service, ms-bflow.service, ms-cloudlicensing.service.

Run
Check algosec-ms.service status
systemctl status algosec-ms.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Check ms-batch-application.service status
systemctl status ms-batch-application.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Step 3
Step focus

Confirm the customer-facing failing path still maps to the expected local target port for that microservice.

Run
Check config mapping
sed -n '1,160p' /etc/httpd/conf.d/algosec-ms.conf
Healthy means: The expected proxy or service mapping is visible in /etc/httpd/conf.d/algosec-ms.conf.
Why this check matters
  • This checks whether the expected mapping still exists in the service config.
  • Use it to confirm the route, port, or target value is still what the application expects.
Step 4
Step focus

Confirm ms-configuration.service is healthy before assuming the issue is isolated to the single feature path.

Run
Check ms-configuration.service status
systemctl status ms-configuration.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Step 5
Step focus

Collect the relevant ms-* service status or error snippet and decide whether the issue stays in the target service or moves to shared dependencies like httpd.service.

Run
Review ms-batch-application.service logs
journalctl -u ms-batch-application.service -n 50 --no-pager
Healthy means: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.
Why this check matters
  • Recent service logs are often the fastest way to find the real failure clue.
  • Focus on startup errors, permission errors, heap errors, dependency failures, and repeated retries.
  • Use this after the status check when the service looks up but still behaves badly.

Messaging and Data

Use this when jobs stop, queues back up, or data actions fail.

Back to top
Step 1
Step focus

Confirm the customer symptom is really a stuck job, stalled event, or data-backed action failure instead of a pure UI-path issue.

Step 2
Step focus

Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present before assuming the issue is only downstream data state.

Run
Check activemq.service status
systemctl status activemq.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Check broker listener
ss -lntp | grep -E ':61616\b'
Healthy means: A listening socket is present for 61616.
Why this check matters
  • This command shows whether Linux is listening on the expected port.
  • `LISTEN` means a process has opened the port and is waiting for connections.
  • If the port is missing, the service may be down, starting slowly, or bound to the wrong place.
Step 3
Step focus

Confirm postgresql.service is active and that the customer environment shows no obvious database connectivity or state error.

Run
Check postgresql.service status
systemctl status postgresql.service --no-pager
Healthy means: Unit is loaded and active (running).
Why this check matters
  • This command asks systemd whether the service is known and running right now.
  • `Loaded` means the unit file exists on the server. `Active (running)` means the service is up now.
  • `Main PID` means systemd still sees a live main process for the service.
Step 4
Step focus

Review the visible queue, backlog, or stuck-job signal and decide whether the problem stays in broker/data infrastructure or should move back to a feature-specific domain.

Run
Review activemq.service logs
journalctl -u activemq.service -n 50 --no-pager
Healthy means: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.
Why this check matters
  • Recent service logs are often the fastest way to find the real failure clue.
  • Focus on startup errors, permission errors, heap errors, dependency failures, and repeated retries.
  • Use this after the status check when the service looks up but still behaves badly.
Review postgresql.service logs
journalctl -u postgresql.service -n 50 --no-pager
Healthy means: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.
Why this check matters
  • Recent service logs are often the fastest way to find the real failure clue.
  • Focus on startup errors, permission errors, heap errors, dependency failures, and repeated retries.
  • Use this after the status check when the service looks up but still behaves badly.

Reference

aff-boot.service

route local listener ports 1989

ssh-connections-monitor.service

No direct path summary captured yet.

ms-batch-application.service

No direct path summary captured yet.

ms-bflow.service

No direct path summary captured yet.

ms-cloudlicensing.service

No direct path summary captured yet.

Known Unknowns
  • Only first-pass config and log checkpoints are collected in this slice.
  • Dependency mapping between AlgoSec services is not yet collected directly.
